era_context: Late Bronze Age, mid-18th Dynasty Egypt under Thutmose III's reign (c. 1479–1425 BCE). The New Kingdom was at its military and territorial zenith. In Mesopotamia, the Kassite dynasty controlled Babylonia. The Hittite Empire dominated Anatolia under Tudhaliya II. In the Aegean, Mycenaean Greece was in the Late Helladic II period. This was an era of imperial consolidation, monumental building, and long-distance trade networks.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records; archaeological evidence sparse for this specific year)
Northern Europe (pre-literate societies; no year-specific events attested)
East Asia (Chinese records exist but are not well-synchronized with Egyptian chronology for this period)
Mesoamerica (no written records for this specific year; Maya civilization not yet emerged)

events.title: Thutmose III's military campaigns in the Levant
events.description: Thutmose III continued his systematic campaigns to consolidate Egyptian control over the Levantine territories. These campaigns, documented in the Karnak temple inscriptions, were part of his broader strategy to expand and maintain Egyptian hegemony in the region. The exact year-by-year sequence of his campaigns remains debated among Egyptologists, but the mid-1430s BCE fall within the period of his most intensive military activity. These campaigns secured tribute routes and prevented Mitanni expansion into Egyptian-controlled territories.
key_figures: Amenhotep II (co-regent or successor, depending on chronology)

sources.name: Karnak Temple Inscriptions (Annals of Thutmose III)

events.certainty_note: The Karnak annals provide detailed campaign records, but the exact year-by-year dating is uncertain due to gaps in the inscription and scholarly disagreement over the length of Thutmose III's reign. Dating to 1436 BCE is conventional but could vary by ±5 years.
cross_references: Related to broader Thutmose III military campaigns (c. 1479–1425 BCE)
Part of Egyptian-Mitanni rivalry in the Levant

events.title: Possible Hittite consolidation under Tudhaliya II
events.description: Tudhaliya II (c. 1460–1440 BCE) was consolidating Hittite power in Anatolia during this period. While no specific event is attested for 1436 BCE, this year falls within his reign, during which he expanded Hittite influence and reorganized the kingdom's administrative structure. The Hittites were emerging as a major imperial power, rivaling Egypt and Mitanni for regional dominance.

sources.name: Hittite royal inscriptions and later compilations (Hittite State Archives)

events.certainty_note: Tudhaliya II's reign is dated c. 1460–1440 BCE, placing 1436 BCE within it, but no specific events are attested for this year. The dating of Hittite chronology in this period remains uncertain (±10–15 years possible).

disconfirming_evidence: The exact dating of Thutmose III's individual campaigns remains contested. Some scholars argue for a shorter reign (c. 1479–1425 BCE), while others propose a longer co-regency with Hatshepsut that would shift the dating of specific campaigns. The Karnak annals, while detailed, do not provide absolute dates and must be synchronized with other chronological systems. Additionally, the identification of specific Levantine sites mentioned in the annals with modern archaeological locations is sometimes disputed.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1436 BCE is heavily dependent on Egyptian temple inscriptions and Hittite archives, both of which are elite, state-sponsored sources. Egyptian records emphasize military victories and royal piety; Hittite records focus on royal legitimacy and territorial claims. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and most of the Americas are entirely absent from the written record for this year. East Asian chronology (particularly Chinese dating) is not well-synchronized with Egyptian chronology, creating uncertainty in global synchronization. Archaeological evidence from the Levant provides some corroboration but is often fragmentary.

graph_edges.note: Thutmose III's Levantine campaigns were a continuous process spanning decades
Hittite consolidation under Tudhaliya II occurred simultaneously with Egyptian expansion under Thutmose III
